EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved through the region ahead of a cold front. This upper level energy sparked strong to severe thunderstorms across western Connecticut during the early afternoon hours of Saturday, August 3rd, 2019. As a result of these storms, multiple trees and wires were downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and damaged a house on Highmark Road. This prompted a massive response by local firefighters. There were occupants in the house when the lightning strike occurred but no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Midwest storm redeveloped along the Mid Atlantic coast on the 12th and moves past Southern New England by the morning of the 13th.  This storm brought up to 4.5 inches of snow and one-quarter inch of ice to Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 6:32 AM EST a trained spotter in East Farmington Heights reported ice accumulation of one-quarter inch. Reports froms Burlington and North Granby indicated one-eighth of an inch of ice. There were no reports of damage, but these amounts normally indicate some transportation issues and possible power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 7:08 AM EST a trained spotter in Somers reported ice accumulation of one-eighth inch. There were no reports of damage, but these amounts normally indicate some transportation issues and possible power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure from the Deep South moved along the South Coast of New England on January 20.  This brought snow and rain to Northern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow fell on Windham County.  An amateur radio operator in Pomfret reported one-quarter inch of ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure center passing through New York State on January 1 brought strong west winds to Massachusetts on January 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 2:18 PM EST a tree fell on wires on Upper Downs Road in Killingly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted northwestern Connecticut on January 19th and 20th. Low pressure formed over the lower Mississippi Valley and lifted northeast across the Tennessee Valley during the night of the 19th before moving along the Mason-Dixon line on the 20th. Strong rising motion occurred as southerly winds aloft transported moist air northward, which was lifted over the cold airmass in place over northwestern Connecticut. Snowfall broke out during the afternoon of the 19th, continuing heavy at times into the evening hours. The strong southerly flow brought above-freezing air aloft into the region, causing the snow to turn to sleet and freezing rain overnight into the early morning of the 20th before turning back to snow. Snow and sleet totals ranged from three to six inches with ice accretion of one to three tenths of an inch due to freezing rain. The freezing rain resulted in a handful of power outages over far southern parts of the county.||Frigid temperatures followed the snow for Monday and Tuesday, January 21st-22nd, with wind chills falling to -15 to -30F. The cold weather prompted the closing of schools and the opening of warming shelters across the region.

An arctic cold front tracked through northwestern Connecticut during the afternoon of the 30th, accompanied by a heavy snow squall. Frigid air poured in behind this front along with strong westerly winds. Wind chills the morning of the 31st fell to 15 to 30 degrees below zero. The wind chills prompted many schools to close or delay opening.

Snow overspread northwestern Connecticut during the afternoon and evening of March 3rd ahead of a strengthening coastal low pressure system. The snow became moderate to heavy at times overnight before ending just before sunrise on the 4th. Snowfall totals ranged from around 5 inches in the northern portion of Litchfield County to near 11 inches in the southern portion. The snow caused some schools to be delayed or cancelled on the 4th.

A strong cold front moved quickly across the region late on the 31st.  Ahead of the front it was a warm and very windy night, with many downed trees and power lines from strong south-southwest wind gusts. A 'fine line' of showers and isolated thunderstorms also helped mix down very strong winds from aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1151 PM EST, the Willimantic Airport measured a wind gust to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure from the Deep South moved along the South Coast of New England on January 20.  This brought snow and rain to Northern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to seven inches of snow fell on Hartford County.  Spotters in Manchester and East Farmington reported ice accumulations of one-quarter inch.  At 10:25 AM EST power lines were reported down on Norton Lane in Berlin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure from the Deep South moved along the South Coast of New England on January 20.  This brought snow and rain to Northern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow fell on Tolland County.  An amateur radio operator in Vernon reported ice accumulation of one-quarter inch.  At 10:25 AM EST a tree and wires were down on the Hartford Turnpike in Vernon.  At 1 PM EST a tree and wires were down on Seagraves Road in Coventry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west to northwest winds trailing the January 20th storm drew cold air across Southern New England early on January 21st and caused wind chill values of 25 below zero or colder in Northern Connecticut through midday. The wind diminished during the afternoon and evening of of January 21st allowing wind chill values to become less extreme, in the teens below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System platform at Bradley International Airport reported wind chill values as low as 28 degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure tracked from the southeast United States on Saturday January 19, 2019 to the northeast on Sunday January 20, 2019. The low produced moderate coastal flooding along the shores of New Haven County during the morning high tide cycle of the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The moderate coastal flood threshold of 9.2 ft MLLW was touched at the NOS tidal gauge at New Haven from 2019-01-20 09:54 to 2019-01-20 10:12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving north from the Gulf of Mexico brought strong south winds and heavy rain to Connecticut on January 24th. The storm moved off through the Maritimes by the next morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1:15 PM EST a tree was reported down on Snake Meadow Road in Moosup.  This caused a power outage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred ahead of and behind low pressure and cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Bridgeport CT measured a 39 mph sustained wind at 1228 pm. A mesonet station 2 miles southwest of Bridgeport measured a sustained wind of 32 mph at 1135 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred ahead of and behind low pressure and cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Groton New London Airport measured a 39 mph wind gust at 231 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front initiated a broken line of snow squalls to the region. The snow squalls moved east across southern Connecticut during the evening commute. The snow squalls weakened as they moved into southeast Connecticut.  While snowfall accumulations were generally under an inch, the squalls brought strong winds and whiteout conditions. Traffic was brought to a standstill during the squalls and created dangerous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls quickly moved through bringing whiteout conditions, strong winds, and dangerous driving conditions. A trained spotter reported 0.5 inches of snowfall in nearby Woodbridge, CT in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to locally heavy rain associated with an area of low pressure resulted in rainfall amounts of 1-3 inches across much of southern Connecticut. Runoff from this rainfall later resulted in minor flooding on the Yantic River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yantic River at Yantic, CT rose above its flood stage of 9.0 feet at 8:00pm EST on January 24th, crested at a height of 9.4 feet at 9:30pm EST, and fell back below flood stage at 12:00am EST on January 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure tracking from the southeast United States on Saturday January 19, 2019 to the northeast on Sunday January 20, 2019. An arctic air mass was located across New England as the storm system approached the region. A warm front associated with the low moved north along the coast early on the morning of January 20 changing snow to sleet and then liquid precipitation. The warmer air battled the arctic air advecting into interior southern Connecticut. However, temperatures remained below freezing through much of the winter storm across creating a significant ice accretion on top of snow accumulation. Many roads were closed due to downed trees and power lines. Several towns were without power for about 24 hours. The Middletown police reported a subcontractor for Eversource was killed by a falling tree on Millbrook Road in Middletown. One injury also occurred in Bethany due to a falling tree limb.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters, CoCoRaHS, and broadcast media reported 3 to 5 inches of snow. The Danbury Airport ASOS reported 0.37 inches of ice accretion. The combination of snow accumulation and ice accretion led to significant impacts across northern Fairfield county. Broadcast media reported multiple road closures in Newtown due to trees and wires down from ice accretion.

The warmer air battled the arctic air advecting into interior southern Connecticut. Many roads were closed due to downed trees and power lines. Several towns were without power for about 24 hours. The Middletown police reported a subcontractor for Eversource was killed by a falling tree on Millbrook Road in Middletown. One injury also occurred in Bethany due to a falling tree limb.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported about 0.7 inches of ice accretion in the Bethany.  The Meriden Airport ASOS reported 0.57 inches of ice accretion. Ice accretion amounts ranged from 0.25 to 0.5 in Hamden and Wallingford. Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches. Many roads were closed due to trees and wires down from ice accretion. The town of Bethany was without power for about 24 hours. One injury occurred in Bethany due to a falling tree limb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a moderate snowfall event on January 19-20, a strong low pressure system tracking through southern Canada ushered in an unseasonably warm and moist airmass on January 24th. Temperatures surged into the 40s to mid-50s. Steady rainfall occurred during much of the 24th as a secondary low pressure system developed over the Mid- Atlantic and tracked into southern New England. One to two inches of rainfall occurred over northwestern Connecticut. The combination of the rainfall along with the mild temperatures melting some of the snow resulted in isolated poor drainage and small stream flooding. In addition, the release of an ice jam on the Housatonic River caused flooding near Kent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Housatonic River south of Kent broke and caused flooding of adjacent lowlands to develop rapidly on the 25th. The floodwaters covered Route 7 and resulted in its closure from Route 341 to Bull's Bridge. One family was evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred behind low pressure and cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Bridgeport Airport measured a sustained wind of 33 mph at 449 pm. A 44 mph gust was measured around that time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred behind low pressure and cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Groton New London Airport measured a 36 mph gust at 535 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed along a warm front and ahead of an upper level trough during the evening of the 29th, continuing past midnight before ending during the early morning hours of the 30th. Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across northwestern Connecticut.

Snow developed along a warm front and ahead of an upper level trough during the evening of the 29th, continuing past midnight before ending during the early morning hours of the 30th. Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across northwestern Connecticut.
Strong winds occurred behind low pressure and strong cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 550 pm in the town of Portland, power lines were reported down on Karen Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening area of low pressure tracked across the region on the evening of October 16th. Several bands of moderate to heavy rain moved across the region in an 8-9 hour time period, with numerous reports of 3-6 inches of rainfall resulting in isolated flash flooding in New London County. A CoCoRaHS observer three miles south-southwest of Central Waterford reported a rainfall total of 6.14 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Bank Street in New London with the road impassable in areas with over a foot of running water in sections.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching storm initially produced moderate coastal flooding across parts of southwest CT on the 27th. After the storm passed, easterly winds as high pressure built into New England also produced moderate coastal flooding there on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak water level of 10.6 ft MLLW occurred at the USGS tidal gauge at Stamford from 2019-10-27 11:00 EDT to 2019-10-27 11:30 EDT. The moderate coastal flood threshold of 10.0 ft MLLW was exceeded from 2019-10-27 10:00 EDT to 2019-10-27 12:30 EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred behind a strong cold front and deepening low pressure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at New Haven Airport measured a 55 mph wind gust at 1142 am. At lighthouse point, the mesonet station measured a 50 mph wind gust around 110 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching storm initially produced moderate coastal flooding across parts of southwest CT on the 27th. After the storm passed, easterly winds as high pressure built into New England also produced moderate coastal flooding there on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak water level of 10.1 ft MLLW occurred at the USGS tidal gauge at Stamford at 2019-10-29 12:30 EDT. The moderate coastal flood threshold of 10.0 ft MLLW was exceeded from 2019-10-29 12:00 EDT to 2019-10-29 13:00 EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass that had infiltrated the region during the last two days of January persisted into the morning of February 1st. Wind chills fell to 10 to 20 degrees below zero. The wind chills prompted many schools to close or delay opening.

Widespread rainfall fell across the region on Friday, February 8th, 2019 as a low pressure system and cold front moved through New York and western New England. Strong winds developed behind the cold front, resulting in scattered power outages into the morning of Saturday, February 9th, 2019. Peak wind gusts up to around 50 mph were recorded.
The first frost and freeze of the season fell upon the area during the early morning hours of Saturday, October 5th, 2019. Low temperatures ranged from the mid 20s to low 30s.
The first frost and freeze of the season fell upon the area during the early morning hours of Saturday, October 5th, 2019. Low temperatures ranged from the mid 20s to low 30s.

Strong to damaging westerly winds developed over eastern New York and western New England on Sunday, February 24th into Monday, February 25th, in association with a deep low pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes. Gusts in excess of 50 mph were common across the area. Numerous power outages and downed trees occurred as a result of the winds.

Gusts in excess of 50 mph were common across the area. Numerous power outages and downed trees occurred as a result of the winds.

A storm the developed over the southern states moved up the coast the night of the 3rd and passed offshore of Nantucket the morning of the 4th. The storm then moved off to the northeast. This brought heavy snow to much of Southern New England. Highest snowfall amounts occurred from Northeast Connecticut to Boston Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From six to twelve inches of snow fell on Hartford County.

This brought snow and wind to Connecticut, with the heaviest amounts found in Tolland County.  West winds trailing the storm reached 30 mph with gusts to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From six to eight inches of snow fell on Tolland County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region triggering strong to severe thunderstorms across Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail of 0.75 inches in diameter reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed across the southeast on Sunday March 3, 2019 and then tracked  off the Middle Atlantic coast early on Monday March 4, 2019. The low moved just inside the 40N/70W benchmark and continued out to sea. The low brought a widespread snowfall to Connecticut with the heaviest accumulations occurring across the interior. Snow mixed with rain near the southeast coast, reducing accumulations. Much of the significant snow occurred overnight with improved conditions during the Monday morning commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS, trained spotters, Broadcast media, and the public reported 8 to 12 inches of snow.

A heat wave gripped northwestern Connecticut from July 19th through the 21st. Temperatures soared to the mid-90s with dewpoints in the low to mid-70s. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system slowly moved across southeast Massachusetts and into the Gulf of Maine during this period.  Six to as much as 18 inches fell in northern Connecticut (highest amounts near the Massachusetts border) by the morning of December 3.  A heavy snow band formed overnight on December 2-3 in Hartford and Tolland Counties in northern Connecticut, which produced several inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Final snow totals ranged from 8 to 12 inches in southern Hartford County to 12 to 18 inches in northern Hartford County.  A trained spotter in North Granby, which had two heavy bands (one on the evening of the 1st and the other overnight on the 2nd/3rd) reported 18.0 inches.

A heavy snow band formed overnight on December 2-3 in Hartford and Tolland Counties in northern Connecticut, which produced several inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Final snow totals ranged from 5 to 9 inches in southern Tolland County to 10 to 17 inches in northern Tolland County. A trained spotter in Somers reported 17.0 inches.  Other sample totals included 14.6 inches in Staffordville, 9.3 inches in Coventry, and only 5.1 inches in Hebron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through southern New England in the evening.  It was preceded by a strong upper level short wave, which tapped very unstable air to produce severe thunderstorms in CT and MA.  An EF-1 tornado occurred in Tolland County in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado moved through a portion of Tolland County between 540 and 549 PM EDT (440 and 449 PM EST), with maximum estimated wind speeds of 85-90 mph.  The tornado traveled in a southeast direction and had a discontinuous path.  It first touched down on North River Road in Coventry, then went through the Skungamaug River Golf Club on Folly Lane (where it damaged many trees).  A tree and wires were down on Merrow Road.  It then lifted before touching down again near Brigham Tavern Road, where it then crossed U.S. Route 44 near Depot Road in Mansfield.  The tornado lifted after going across a corn field near the intersection of Plains Road and Stafford Road (Route 32).  The majority of the damage was to trees.  Only very minor damage to buildings was noted, with a sign at a daycare center on Depot Road blown over.  Scattered branches and wires were downed on Ravine Road, just beyond where the tornado had lifted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm impacted eastern New York and western New England December 1-3, 2019. Snowfall began during the late morning and early afternoon of the 1st ahead of a weakening low pressure system approaching from the western Great Lakes. As the primary low pressure system weakened, it transferred its energy to a coastal low, which strengthened as it remained nearly stationary for much of the 2nd. This allowed snow to continue to accumulate throughout the day. As the low finally pulled away late on the 2nd into the early morning hours of the 3rd, one final heavy snow band crossed the area before the snow finally pulled out prior to daybreak on the 3rd. Amounts of 8-18 were common, except for southern portions of Litchfield County due to warmer temperatures and mixed precipitation.

A progressive storm system approached the area from the southwest late Monday night, spreading a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain across western Connecticut. The wintry mix came to an end late Tuesday evening, with snow, sleet and freezing rain totals less than two inches.

A complex winter storm brought a mix of rain, snow, sleet and freezing rain to the region from the evening of Sunday, December 29th, lingering through the morning of Tuesday, December 31st, 2019. Heavy accumulations of ice impacted portions of western Connecticut during this time frame. There were approximately 2,000 people without power due to a combination of icy conditions and strong winds bringing down trees and power lines. Additionally, two rounds of thunder and lightning occurred with this dynamic system, producing 1 inch hail in Colebrook, CT. A spotter reported nearly 0.3 of ice accumulation in several locations, mainly above 1300 feet in elevation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system moving from Ohio to Lake Ontario drew anomalous warmth and moisture northward on Halloween, with temperatures surging into the 60s and 70s over western New England. Moderate rainfall and strong winds developed across the region as a result of this low pressure system. Winds gusted as high as 45 miles per hour and up to an inch of rain fell in western Connecticut.

Widespread precipitation developed in association with a coastal low pressure system during the early morning hours of the 24th. While lower elevations saw mainly rain, some of the higher elevations in the Litchfield Hills saw a wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain changing to snow. Accumulations were generally light, but the wintry precipitation resulted in slippery travel conditions with some accidents reported. The wintry precipitation ended in the evening of the 24th.
